article_title: I'd vibe code any day, but I only trust these models with UI design
core_thesis: While vibe coding accelerates development, most AI models produce generic, soulless UIs due to reliance on training data defaults. Only a few models—Claude, Gemini, and ChatGPT—demonstrate genuine design sense, with Claude being the best for aesthetic judgment, contextual memory, and adherence to design systems. The key to good AI-generated UI is not just the model but also providing a clear design brief before prompting.
detailed_summary: The article explores the author's experience with vibe coding—using AI to generate code from natural language descriptions—and the common pitfalls in UI design. The author notes that most vibe-coded UIs look generic (blue-to-purple gradients, centered cards, excessive padding) because AI defaults to popular patterns in its training data. While functional, these UIs lack aesthetic hierarchy, rhythm, and contrast, leading to poor user experience. The author tested three major AI models—Claude (Sonnet 4.6 and Opus 4.7), Gemini, and ChatGPT—with an identical prompt to build a SaaS analytics dashboard in React with dark mode. Claude produced the most detailed, bug-free UI with animations and thoughtful UX improvements. Gemini performed well when given reference material but was inconsistent from scratch. ChatGPT delivered clean, functional designs but lacked inspiration and struggled with complex projects. The author recommends a workflow: first create a design brief (as JSON or Markdown) specifying typography, color palette, and feel, then hand it to the AI. This extra step ensures the AI works within defined bounds rather than guessing. Ultimately, Claude is the top choice for UI design, Gemini excels with visual references, and ChatGPT is best for fast iteration. The article concludes that vibe coding is only as good as the design decisions the developer brings beforehand.

entities.name: Claude (Sonnet 4.6 / Opus 4.7)
entities.role: AI model by Anthropic, rated best for UI design due to aesthetic judgment, contextual memory, and adherence to design systems.

entities.name: Gemini
entities.role: Google's AI model, good for UI when provided with reference images or existing designs, but inconsistent from scratch.

entities.name: ChatGPT
entities.role: OpenAI's AI model, delivers clean and functional UIs but lacks inspiration and struggles with complex projects.

entities.name: Yadullah Abidi
entities.role: Author and MakeUseOf staff writer, computer science graduate, full-stack developer, and cybersecurity journalist.

key_data_points: Most vibe-coded UIs share tell-tale signs: same fonts, icons, blue-to-purple gradient backgrounds, centered cards with excessive padding—because AI defaults to popular patterns in training data.
A functional button can feel like it floats above the page; spacing can be technically valid but visually haywire. These issues degrade user experience even if code passes tests.
Claude Sonnet 4.6 and Opus 4.7 produced the most detailed, bug-free UI with animations and UX improvements when given an identical prompt to build a SaaS analytics dashboard.
Gemini performed well when provided with reference images or existing designs, but from scratch it often produced inconsistent outputs with edge-case issues.
ChatGPT stayed disciplined within the spec and delivered clean, functional UIs, but they were rarely inspired and it lost context in complex projects.

The recommended workflow: create a design brief as a JSON or Markdown file specifying typography, color palette, and feel before prompting the AI. This extra step significantly improves output quality.
Claude is the best overall for UI design; Gemini is best when you have visual references; ChatGPT is best for fast iteration workflows.
methodology_steps: Step 1: Define your UI's visual direction before prompting. Decide on typography, color palette, and the general feeling you want.
Step 2: Create a design brief as a JSON or Markdown file that captures your design decisions. This file will be handed to the AI model.
Step 3: Choose the AI model based on your needs: Claude for best aesthetic judgment and contextual memory, Gemini if you have reference images, ChatGPT for fast iteration.

Step 4: Provide the design brief to the AI along with your prompt. Ask it to work within the defined bounds (e.g., 'Build a SaaS analytics dashboard in React with dark mode, using the attached design brief.').
Step 5: Review the generated UI for consistency, spacing, contrast, and responsiveness. Iterate by refining the brief or providing additional context.
conclusions: Claude (Sonnet 4.6 and Opus 4.7) is the best AI model for UI design because it produces interfaces that feel designed rather than assembled from defaults, with strong aesthetic judgment, contextual memory, and adherence to design systems.
Gemini is a strong choice when you have reference images or existing designs to guide it, but it is inconsistent when building from scratch without visual references.
ChatGPT is reliable for fast, functional UI generation but lacks the design inspiration needed for complex or visually ambitious projects.
The quality of AI-generated UI depends heavily on the developer's upfront design decisions. Providing a clear design brief (JSON/Markdown) before prompting dramatically improves results.
Vibe coding is a powerful accelerator, but it cannot replace human design thinking. The developer must bring a clear visual direction and communicate it effectively to the AI.
personal_relevance_summary: As a programmer and Red Teamer, this article is valuable because it provides a practical comparison of AI models for UI generation, which can be used to rapidly prototype phishing pages, C2 dashboards, or tool interfaces. The recommended workflow—creating a design brief before prompting—can be adapted to generate convincing, custom UIs for social engineering or covert channels. The article also highlights the importance of aesthetic judgment in UI, which is often overlooked in offensive security tooling. By leveraging Claude's superior design sense, you can create more believable and effective interfaces for your Red Team operations.
extra_advice: Consider using Claude to generate UI components for your Red Team tools (e.g., fake login pages, admin panels). Create a design brief that mimics the target's branding (colors, fonts, layout) and feed it to Claude to produce a convincing replica. This can save hours of manual HTML/CSS work and improve the realism of your phishing simulations.
